back_translation: Then the high priest asked, "Are these charges true?"
Stephen then said, "Brothers and fathers, listen to me. The God of glory appeared to Abraham our ancestor, while he was still in Mesopotamia, before he settled in the city of Haran.
And He said to him, 'Go out from your land and your kindred, and to the land that I will show you.'
So Abraham went out from the land of the Chaldeans and went to settle in the city of Haran. Afterward, when his father died, God moved him from there to this land, which you all dwell in at present.

He did not give an inheritance in this land to him at all, not even a space the size of a foot-sole. But He promised that He would give this land as a possession to him and to the descendants who would succeed after him, even though at that time he had no child yet.
God said thus, that his descendants would be foreigners in a land of others, and would be oppressed into slavery and persecuted for four hundred years.
God said, 'But I will judge the nation that forces them to be slaves, and afterward they will come out and serve me in this place.'

Then God gave the covenant of circumcision to him, and thus Abraham fathered Isaac and had him circumcised on the eighth day. And Isaac was the father of Jacob, and Jacob was the father of the twelve ancestors of Israel.
Those ancestors grew jealous of Joseph, and sold him as a slave to Egypt. But God dwelt with him.
And God rescued him from all troubles, showed favor and gave wisdom to him in the royal-eyes of Pharaoh king of Egypt, who set him as ruler over Egypt and all his household.
Then a famine arose covering all Egypt and Canaan along with great suffering, and our ancestors could not find sustenance-food.
When Jacob heard that there was grain-food in Egypt, he sent our ancestors to go for the first time.
On the second journey, Joseph revealed himself to his brothers, and Joseph's family became known to Pharaoh.
Then Joseph sent people to call Jacob his father and all the kinsmen, totaling seventy-five persons, to come.
So Jacob went down to Egypt, and later he himself and our ancestors died there.
Their bodies were moved to the city of Shechem, and were buried in the tomb that Abraham had bought with a sum of money from the sons of Hamor in the city of Shechem.
When the time of the promise that God had given to Abraham drew near, our people in Egypt prospered, multiplied, and increased in number.
Until another king arose to rule Egypt, who did not know Joseph.
This king used craftiness against our nation, and oppressed our ancestors to abandon their infants outside, so that they would not survive.
At that very time, Moses was born, and was a child beautiful in the royal-sight of God. He received care in his father's house for three months.
When he was abandoned outside, Pharaoh's daughter picked him up and raised him as if he were her own son.
So Moses received education in all the branches-of-knowledge of the Egyptians, and was a man of power both in speech and in action of his.
When Moses reached the age of forty, it arose in his heart to go visit his brothers, the descendants of Israel.
When he saw one person being oppressed, he went in to defend and to avenge on behalf of the one who was being mistreated, by striking-to-death that Egyptian.
He thought that his brothers would understand that God was using his hand to help them to be saved, but they did not understand.
The next day he went in to two Israelites who were fighting, and attempted to reconcile them by saying, 'You two, you are brothers already; why do you wrong one another?'
But the one who wronged his neighbor pushed Moses away and said, 'Who made you ruler and judge over us?'
'Do you want to kill me like you killed the Egyptian yesterday?'
When he heard such words, Moses fled, and became a foreigner in the land of Midian. There he fathered two sons.
When forty years had passed, an angel appeared to Moses in the wilderness of Mount Sinai, amid flames of fire in a bush.
Moses saw and marveled at that vision. When he came near to look closely, the royal-voice of the Lord came to him, saying,
'I am the God of your ancestors, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.' Moses trembled with fear and dared not look.
The Lord said to him, 'Take off your sandals, because the place where you stand is holy.'
'I have seen the suffering of my people who are in Egypt most clearly, and have heard their mournful-cry. I have come down to help them to escape. Now come forth, I will send you to Egypt.'

This very Moses whom they rejected by saying, 'Who made you ruler and judge?' — but God sent this very one to be both ruler and redeemer, through the royal-hand of the angel who appeared to him at that bush.
This very one led them out, by performing wondrous-acts and signs in the land of Egypt, at the Red Sea, and in the wilderness for forty years.
This very Moses is the one who said to the descendants of Israel, 'God will raise up a prophet from among your brothers, like me.'

This one is Moses who was in the assembly of Israel in the wilderness, together with the angel who spoke to him on Mount Sinai, and together with our ancestors. He was the one who received royal-speech which is living to pass on to us.
But our ancestors did not want to obey Moses. On the contrary, they rejected him and in their heart turned back to Egypt.
They said to Aaron, 'Make for us various gods, which will go before us, because this Moses who brought us out from the land of Egypt, we do not know what has happened to him.'
In those days they made an image of a calf, and brought a sacrifice to offer to that idol, and rejoiced together in the output-work of their hands.

But God turned His royal-face away from them, and released them to serve the army of heaven, as is written in the book of the prophets, 'O descendants of Israel, for forty years in the wilderness, did you offer animal-sacrifices and sacrificial-offerings to me?
You carried the tabernacle of the god Molech, and the star of the god Rephan, the idols you built to worship. Therefore I will deport you into exile beyond Babylon.'
Our ancestors had the tabernacle of testimony in the wilderness, which was built according to what the One who spoke to Moses commanded to be built according to the pattern that Moses had seen.

Our ancestors who received the tabernacle as an inheritance brought it in with them, under the leadership of Joshua, when they seized the land of various nations, which God drove out from before our ancestors. And that tabernacle remained until the era of David.
David received royal-grace before the royal-face of God, and petitioned to seek a royal-dwelling for the house-descendants of Jacob.
But it was Solomon rather who was the one who built the royal-temple offered to Him.
However, the Most High does not dwell in a place which is built by human hands, as the prophet said,
'Heaven is my throne, and the earth is the resting place for my royal-foot. What kind of royal-temple will you build for me? the Lord says thus. Or where will my dwelling-place be?
Has not my hand made all these things?'
O people stiff-necked, with hearts and ears that have not been circumcised, you all always oppose the Holy Spirit; just as your ancestors did, you yourselves do the same too.

Which of the prophets did your ancestors not persecute? They even killed those who announced in advance the royal-coming of the Righteous One, whom now you all have become betrayers and murderers of that One Himself.
You all received the royal-law which angels were the ones who gave, but you did not keep it.
When those people heard this, they felt cut-in-heart exceedingly, and gnashed their teeth at Stephen.
But Stephen, full of the Holy Spirit, gazed intently up to heaven, and saw the royal-glory of God and saw Jesus standing at the right of the royal-hand of God.
And he said, "Behold, I see the heaven opening, and see the Son of Man standing at the right of the royal-hand of God."
But they shouted loudly and covered their own ears, then together rushed in toward him.
They dragged him out outside the city and began to throw stones at him. As for the witnesses, they laid their cloaks at the feet of a young man named Saul.
While they were throwing stones at Stephen, he cried-out-and-prayed, "O Lord Jesus, please royally receive my soul-spirit."
Then he knelt down and cried out loudly, "O Lord, please do not royally hold this sin against them." When he had said this, he passed-into-sleep.
